topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air hung heavy, thick with the scent of damp earth and decay. Elias stood at the edge of the clearing, his shadow long and distorted in the late afternoon sun. His gaze fixed on the barren patch where the ancient oak used to stand. It was just a stump now, raw and jagged, a stark scar against the muted greens of the surrounding woods. A slow, cold knot tightened in his chest. He traced the outline of the cut with a hesitant finger, the skin rough against the splintered wood. He remembered the pride of that summer, the careful tending of the sapling, the whispered promises made to the young thing. Now, only silence answered. He turned away abruptly, the sound of his own footsteps crunching on fallen leaves sounding unnaturally loud in the stillness.

[story 2]

"Gone." The word was a low rasp in Maya's throat. She didn't move from the spot, her shoulders slumped as if the weight of the world had settled directly onto her spine. Her eyes, usually bright, were dull, fixed on the truncated trunk. She ran a hand through her hair, tangling the strands as if trying to physically untangle the confusion swirling inside her. Every muscle in her jaw was clenched tight. She remembered the effort, the sheer will it took to coax life from the soil, and now, that effort felt utterly wasted, a foolish expenditure of energy. A shudder ran through her, a deep, internal tremor that had nothing to do with the cool evening air.

[story 3]

He walked the perimeter of the clearing three times, each circuit a deliberate act of self-punishment. The silence pressed in, suffocating, amplifying the frantic rhythm of his own heartbeat against his ribs. When he finally reached the spot, he didn't look at the stump immediately. He knelt down, pressing his forehead against the rough bark, seeking some residual connection to the past. His breath hitched, shallow and quick. A strange, hollow ache settled deep beneath his sternum. He could almost hear the phantom rustle of leaves, the youthful energy he had poured into nurturing that small life, now utterly extinguished. He rose without a word, the movement stiff, and walked toward the distant road, his pace measured and heavy.
